purpose: Atomic grammar rules for RAG-based Mirad <-> English translation.
schema_note: Each object is one retrieval unit. Embed title + rule.description + examples + retrieval_tags.

orthography_phonology.title: Mirad uses the Roman alphabet without diacritics for native words

rule.description: Native Mirad words are written with the Latin alphabet and no diacritics. Capitalization is used like English for sentence starts, proper nouns, and acronyms.
pseudocode: if token.is_sentence_initial or token.is_proper_noun or token.is_acronym:

orthography_phonology.title: Use a colon before direct quotations

rule.description: Mirad uses a colon before direct speech where English often uses a comma.

orthography_phonology.title: Consonant letters map one-to-one to sounds

rule.description: Native Mirad has no silent consonants and no native consonant digraphs. Notable values: x = English sh, c = ch, j = zh, g is always hard, s is always unvoiced.
pseudocode: do_not_apply English digraph or silent-letter rules

orthography_phonology.title: Every syllable contains exactly one vowel nucleus

rule.description: Each Mirad syllable contains one vowel. Glided vowels with y or w count as one complex vowel for syllabification and stress.
pseudocode: split word so each syllable has exactly one nucleus
treat final/post-vocalic y or w as part_of preceding complex vowel when applicable

examples.analysis: ay-ma; ay is one complex vowel

orthography_phonology.title: Stress falls on the last non-final vowel

rule.description: Stress is regular and not phonemic. In words with more than one syllable, stress occurs on the last non-final vowel, including complex glided vowels.

nouns.title: Common singular nouns end in a consonant

rule.description: In native Mirad, singular common nouns normally end in a consonant. Proper nouns may end in vowels.
pseudocode: if noun.is_common and noun.number == singular and noun.is_native:

examples.analysis: singular common noun ending in consonant
proper noun may end in vowel

rule.description: A singular countable common noun becomes plural by suffixing -i. Only the noun is pluralized; articles and adjectives do not agree in number.

examples.mirad: ha via dom → ha via domi
examples.english: the beautiful city → the beautiful cities

nouns.title: Use ha as the invariable definite article

rule.description: Mirad has one definite article, ha. It precedes the noun and any adjectives. It does not change for gender, number, or case. There is no indefinite article.

source.section: Modifying Nouns with the Definite Article

nouns.title: Noun modifiers precede the noun and do not agree

rule.description: Qualitative, quantitative, deictic, and pronominal adjectives precede the noun. There is no gender or number agreement between modifiers and noun.
pseudocode: mirad_np = optional_article + determiners + quantitative_modifiers + descriptive_adjectives + noun

examples.analysis: modifiers precede noun; only tami is plural

nouns.title: Use X bi Y for possession by a named person

rule.description: Possession by a named person is expressed as possessed noun + bi + possessor, equivalent to English 'Y's X' or 'the X of Y'.

examples.english: Mary's house / the house of Mary

nouns.title: Nouns may link by conjunction, preposition, juxtaposition, compounding, or hyphenation

rule.description: Noun relationships may be expressed with prepositions/conjunctions after the head noun, by modifier noun before the head noun, by compounding, or by hyphenating equal/opposed elements.
pseudocode: use modifier_noun + head_noun or compound(modifier, head)

nouns.title: Mark noun gender with w for masculine and y for feminine

rule.description: Nouns are gender-neutral by default. To create a male form, prefix the semantically significant vowel with w. To create a female form, suffix that vowel with y. Agent nouns in -ut use -uyt for female, while the male form usually stays -ut.

examples.analysis: w marks masculine; y marks feminine

rule.description: All descriptive adjectives end in the adjectival marker -a. Adjectives can be derived from qualitative roots, nouns, passive verb stems, and prepositions.
Descriptive adjectives are placed before the nouns they modify.

adjectives_adverbs.title: Use degree quantifiers before adjectives for comparison

rule.description: Comparative and degree meanings are expressed by placing a quantitative determiner before the adjective. vyel introduces the comparison standard, like English 'than' or 'as'.

examples.mirad: Ata tam se ga aga vyel etas.
examples.english: My house is bigger than yours.

rule.description: An adjective can become an abstract quality noun with -n, a thing with that quality with -s, or a person with that quality with -t.

examples.english: good / goodness / good thing / good person
fair / justice / fair thing / fair person

adjectives_adverbs.title: Negate adjective semantics with o-, lo-, or ol-

rule.description: Descriptive adjectives can be negated by prefixing o-. If this creates ambiguity or the adjective already begins with o, lo- or ol- may be used. Stronger polar opposition can use lyo- or ov-.

adjectives_adverbs.title: Form adjective opposites by vowel switching

rule.description: Many base adjectives form semantic contrasts by switching the stem vowel. In i/u pairs, i is positive and u negative. In a/e/o scales, a is positive, e intermediate, and o negative.

adjectives_adverbs.title: Deintensify adjective meaning with a y-glide

rule.description: The semantic strength of many qualitative adjectives can be reduced by adding a y-glide after the ordinal or stem vowel.

adjectives_adverbs.title: Many adverbs are formed with -y from adjectives

rule.description: A descriptive adjective can often be converted into an adverb by adding -y, corresponding to English -ly.
pseudocode: if adjective modifies verb/adjective/clause as manner_or_degree:

source.section: Adverbs / Comparison of Adverbs by Degree

adjectives_adverbs.title: Adverbs use the same degree quantifiers as adjectives

rule.description: Adverbs are compared by placing quantitative degree words before them, parallel to adjective comparison. vyel introduces the comparison standard.

examples.mirad: Dalu ge azay vyel et yafe.
examples.english: Speak as loudly as you can.

pronouns.title: Personal pronouns do not change for case

rule.description: Mirad personal pronouns have the same form whether subject, direct object, indirect object, or object of a preposition. Syntax and prepositions determine function.

pronouns.title: Animate pronouns end in -t and inanimate pronouns end in -s

rule.description: Pronouns ending in -t refer to animate beings; pronouns ending in -s refer to inanimate objects, concepts, or abstractions. English 'it/they/them' must be resolved by antecedent animacy.

pronouns.title: Core personal pronouns encode person and number

rule.description: Core animate personal pronouns are at I/me, et you, it he/she/him/her, yat we/us, yet you plural, yit they/them. Plural personal pronouns generally begin with y.

pronouns.title: Third-person animate pronouns can optionally mark gender

rule.description: The gender-neutral third-person animate pronoun it can become wit for masculine or iyt for feminine. Gender marking is optional and used when needed to distinguish referents.

pronouns.title: Use ot and yot for generic animate reference

rule.description: ot means generic one; yot means people/they in a generic unspecific sense.

examples.english: One must do what is expected.

examples.mirad: Yot de van et vyode hyoj.

pronouns.title: Use aet and ayet for first-plus-second-person hybrid pronouns

rule.description: aet means 'you and I' dual. ayet means 'you people and I' plural. Use only when the situation explicitly combines first and second person.

examples.english: You and I should get married.
You people and I need to stick together.

pronouns.title: Form reflexive pronouns by adding u before final -t

rule.description: Reflexive personal pronouns are formed by placing u before the final -t. Reflexives do not mark gender.

pronouns.title: Prefix ut- to verbs and nouns for self-/auto- meanings

rule.description: The reflexive element ut can be prefixed to verbs or other stems to mean self-, auto-, or reflexive action.

pronouns.title: Form emphatic pronouns with personal pronoun plus reflexive

rule.description: Emphatic personal pronouns are formed by following a personal pronoun with the corresponding reflexive pronoun.

pronouns.title: Use hyuit for animate reciprocal and hyuis for inanimate reciprocal

rule.description: hyuit means one another / each other for sentient beings. hyuis means one another / each other for inanimate objects, concepts, or abstractions. These forms do not express gender or number.

examples.english: Planets sometimes crash into each other.

pronouns.title: Form possessive adjectives and pronouns from personal pronouns

rule.description: Possessive adjectives are formed by adding -a to the pronoun. Possessive pronouns for things are formed with -as singular and -asi plural. Change s to t when the possessed referent is a person.

examples.english: Yours are worth more than mine.

pronouns.title: Translate English it by antecedent type or omit dummy it

rule.description: If English 'it' refers to an animate creature, use it/iyt/wit. If it refers to an inanimate object or abstraction, use is or a deictic determiner. If it is a dummy/expletive subject with no antecedent, omit it in Mirad.

examples.mirad: At eke bay ha epet hosav at iyfe it.
examples.english: I play with the dog because I like it.

examples.mirad: At teasa ha dyezun ay at iyfa is gla.
examples.english: I saw the movie and I liked it a lot.

source.section: Translating various uses of it in English

verbs.title: Verb stems are formed by removing -er from the infinitive

rule.description: Verbs are listed under the present active infinitive ending in -er. The stem is the infinitive minus -er. All conjugation uses this stem. Passive infinitives ending in -wer may be lexicalized, but the stem is still the infinitive minus -er.

verbs.title: Verbs do not change for person or number

rule.description: The same verb form is used for all subjects regardless of person or number. Subject pronouns carry person and number information.
pseudocode: verb_form = conjugate(stem, tense, aspect, mood, voice)

verbs.title: Simple active tense suffixes are -e, -a, -o, -u

rule.description: Simple active present uses stem + -e, past stem + -a, future stem + -o, and atemporal hypothetical/imperative stem + -u.
pseudocode: if aspect == simple and voice == active:
if tense == present and mood == indicative: form = stem + 'e'
if tense == past and mood == indicative: form = stem + 'a'
if tense == future and mood == indicative: form = stem + 'o'
if mood == hypothetical or imperative: form = stem + 'u'

source.section: Simple Aspect / Conjugation in the Simple Active Tenses

verbs.title: Passive voice inserts w before the tense vowel

rule.description: In finite passive forms, the passive marker w is inserted between the verb stem and the final tense/mood vowel. Passive forms translate English 'be/get + past participle' when a patient is subject.
pseudocode: if voice == passive and aspect == simple:

verbs.title: Simple passive forms are stem + w + tense vowel

rule.description: Simple passive present is -we, past -wa, future -wo, and hypothetical -wu after the verb stem.
pseudocode: if aspect == simple and voice == passive:

verbs.title: Active progressive uses stem + ey + tense vowel

rule.description: The active progressive inserts the present aspect vowel e plus buffer y between the stem and final tense/mood vowel: -eye, -eya, -eyo, -eyu.
pseudocode: if aspect == progressive and voice == active:

verbs.title: Passive progressive uses stem + ew + tense vowel

rule.description: The passive progressive inserts e plus passive/buffer w before the final tense/mood vowel: -ewe, -ewa, -ewo, -ewu.
pseudocode: if aspect == progressive and voice == passive:

verbs.title: Active perfect uses stem + ay + tense vowel

rule.description: The active perfect inserts the past aspect vowel a plus buffer y before the final tense/mood vowel: -aye, -aya, -ayo, -ayu.
pseudocode: if aspect == perfect and voice == active:

verbs.title: Passive perfect uses stem + aw + tense vowel

rule.description: The passive perfect inserts a plus passive/buffer w before the final tense/mood vowel: -awe, -awa, -awo, -awu.
pseudocode: if aspect == perfect and voice == passive:

verbs.title: Imminent aspect uses future aspect vowel o plus buffer y/w

rule.description: The imminent/prospective aspect means 'be about to'. It uses future aspect vowel o plus buffer y in active voice or w in passive voice before the final tense vowel. Only indicative mood imminent tenses are normally used.
pseudocode: if aspect == imminent and voice == active:
if aspect == imminent and voice == passive:

verbs.title: Potential aspect uses u plus buffer y/w

rule.description: The potential aspect indicates aptitude or likelihood. It uses u plus buffer y in active voice or w in passive voice before the final tense/mood vowel.
pseudocode: if aspect == potential and voice == active:
if aspect == potential and voice == passive:

examples.english: is apt to do / may do

verbs.title: -u form is conditional with a subject and imperative without one

rule.description: A verb ending in -u is atemporal hypothetical. With an overt subject in a main clause it usually means would. Without a subject it is normally interpreted as an imperative command.

verbs.title: Modal auxiliaries precede infinitives

rule.description: Modal verbs such as wanting, needing, being able, being obliged, liking, and fearing are conjugated as auxiliaries and followed by an infinitive verb in -er.

verbs.title: Hypothetical modal forms soften modal meaning

rule.description: When a modal auxiliary is in the hypothetical -u form, its meaning is softened, similar to English would like, could, ought to.

verbs.title: Post-y-gliding modal stems reduces modal strength

rule.description: Adding a y-glide after the modal stem vowel weakens the force of some modal or affective verbs, such as love to like or must to should.

verbs.title: Transitive verbs take a direct object after the verb

rule.description: A transitive verb can be followed by its direct object. In basic declaratives this follows SVO order.

verbs.title: Some verbs take indirect object before direct object

rule.description: Many common transitive verbs imply a directional preposition. When both indirect and direct objects are present, the indirect object comes before the direct object without an explicit preposition. A prepositional paraphrase can also be used.
pseudocode: if verb.implies_direction and has_indirect_object and has_direct_object:

examples.analysis: indirect object at before direct object hua nyem

source.section: The Object / Verbs of Motion and Communication

verbs.title: Stative verbs take complements rather than objects

rule.description: Stative intransitive verbs such as ser 'to be' may be followed by complements: noun, pronoun, adjective, infinitive, gerund, or clause.

verbs.title: Communication and cognition verbs can take clause objects

rule.description: Verbs of knowing, saying, telling, and informing can take a whole clause as direct object. van can introduce a that-clause, and interrogative deictics can introduce embedded questions.

examples.english: I knew that you would come.

examples.mirad: Duven et te hoa tam se itas?
examples.english: Do you know which house is his?

examples.mirad: At da it hom et tambee.
examples.english: I told him where you live.

verbs.title: Motion and communication verbs may omit inherent directional prepositions

rule.description: Certain verbs contain inherent directionality or positionality and can take an object without a preposition where English might use to/from/in. This includes motion, residence, origin, and communication verbs.

examples.english: I am going to the store.

source.section: Omitting Prepositions after Certain Verbs / Verbs of Motion and Communication

determiners.title: Determiners include deictic, pronominal, quantitative, and numerical types

rule.description: Mirad determiners specify context and may function adjectivally, pronominally, or adverbially. Major types are deictic, pronominal/possessive, quantitative, and numerical.
All deictic determiners begin with h. Following letters specify category such as interrogative, indefinite, proximal, distal, universal, negative, person, thing, place, time, manner, degree, etc.
Interrogative deictic determiners are built with duh- plus a category ending: duhot? who/whom, duhos? what, duhota? whose, duhom? where, duhoj? when, duhoyen? how.

determiners.title: Deictic pronouns use -t for persons, -s for things, and -i for plurals

rule.description: Deictic pronouns ending in -t refer to persons and those ending in -s refer to things. Plural is formed with -i: duhot? who, duhoti? which persons; duhos? what, duhosi? what things.

source.section: Animate Determiners and Personal Pronouns / Pronominalizing Adjectives

determiners.title: Use hia for this and hua for that

rule.description: hia marks proximal reference, this; hua marks distal reference, that. As adjectives they precede the noun. As pronouns they can take -s/-t and plural -i.
pseudocode: if used_as_thing_pronoun: form = base + 'as' or base + 'asi'
if used_as_person_pronoun: form = base + 'at' or base + 'ati'

determiners.title: Use hya, hyaha, and hyawa for every/all/all the/each

rule.description: hya means every/all. hyaha means all the. hyawa means each. Choose based on whether the English quantifier is distributive, collective, or definite collective.

examples.mirad: Hya dom ayse tebixea suni teaxer.
examples.english: Every city has interesting things to see.
All the cities have things of interest.
Each city has something of interest.

determiners.title: hawa means the only; haway means only/solely

rule.description: hawa is an adjectival determiner meaning the only. Adding -y gives haway, an adverb meaning only or solely.

examples.mirad: Hawa toyb nazea ata tezex se et.
examples.english: The only woman worth my attention is you.

rule.description: Either A or B is Hyeawa A ey B. Neither A nor B is Hyeowa A oy B. Both A and B is Hyaewa A ay B. Only A, not B is Hawa A, voy B.
pseudocode: if construction == either_or: use 'Hyeawa' + A + 'ey' + B
if construction == neither_nor: use 'Hyeowa' + A + 'oy' + B
if construction == both_and: use 'Hyaewa' + A + 'ay' + B
if construction == only_not: use 'Hawa' + A + ', voy' + B

determiners.title: Deictic adverbs encode place, time, and manner

rule.description: Correlative deictic adverbs include place: duhom? where, hem somewhere, him here, hum there; time: duhoj? when, hej sometime, hij now, huj then; manner: duhoyen? how, heyen somehow, hiyen this way, huyen that way.

determiners.title: Translate English how according to function

rule.description: English 'how' can be manner adverb duhoyen?, adjective of kind duhoyena?, or degree expression duhonog/duhogla depending on what it modifies.
pseudocode: if how asks quality/kind of noun/event: use 'duhoyena?'
if how asks degree of adjective: use degree form such as 'duhonog' or 'duhogla' + adjective

rule.description: Mirad is normally SVO: subject + verb predicate + object, similar to English and Romance languages.

syntax_word_order.title: Subjects are noun/pronoun phrases with optional modifiers

rule.description: The subject is the entity responsible for the verb's action or state. It can be a noun phrase or pronoun, with modifiers such as deictic adjectives, quantifiers, and descriptive adjectives before the head noun.

examples.english: All cities have things of interest.
examples.analysis: hya domi is subject noun phrase

syntax_word_order.title: Objects and complements follow the verb predicate

rule.description: In ordinary clauses, direct objects and complements follow the finite verb predicate. Indirect objects may precede direct objects with verbs that imply direction.
pseudocode: if indirect_object and direct_object and verb.implies_direction: place indirect_object before direct_object

syntax_word_order.title: Direct discourse follows a reporting clause and colon

rule.description: In direct discourse, a reporting clause can introduce the quoted material with a colon.

prepositions_conjunctions.title: Use ay, ey, and oy for and, or, and but/nor-like exclusion

rule.description: ay links elements as 'and'; ey links alternatives as 'or'; oy can express exclusion/contrast such as 'but' or in neither/nor patterns, depending on construction.
van introduces a subordinate declarative clause corresponding to English 'that', especially after verbs of cognition, saying, or in complement clauses.
pseudocode: introduce_with 'van' when clarity or grammar requires

examples.english: I knew that you would come.
It is sad that she left.

prepositions_conjunctions.title: bi expresses of, possession, and partitive relations

rule.description: bi translates many English of-relations, including possession by named persons and partitives such as a glass of wine.

examples.english: Mary's house / the house of Mary

prepositions_conjunctions.title: bay means with/in the company of

rule.description: bay expresses accompaniment, corresponding to English 'with' in the sense of in the company of.
av corresponds to English 'for' in beneficiary or advantage contexts.

source.section: Cases / Verbs of Motion and Communication

rule.description: boy links a noun phrase to express lack or absence, corresponding to English 'without'.
ayv introduces the topic or subject matter of a noun, speech, writing, or thought, corresponding to English 'about'.

examples.mirad: dyes ayv ha Ea Mira Dropek
examples.english: a book about the Second World War

derivation_word_building.title: Compounds often drop adjective -a and verb -er in prefixes

rule.description: The first two or three letters of a word can serve as prefixes. When used as prefixes, adjective -a and verb -er are often dropped; long noun prefixes may be clipped.

derivation_word_building.title: Use -an/-n to derive abstract quality nouns

rule.description: Abstract quality nouns meaning -ness, -hood, or -ity are commonly formed from adjective stems with -an or -n.

derivation_word_building.title: Use -en to derive gerunds/action nouns from verbs

rule.description: The suffix -en forms present gerunds or action nouns meaning English -ing, -tion, or -ment from verb stems.

derivation_word_building.title: Use -ut for agents and -uyt for female agents

rule.description: Agent nouns meaning one who does X are formed from verb stems with -ut. The female-specific form uses -uyt.

derivation_word_building.title: Use -t for person and -s for thing from adjectives and determiners

rule.description: The suffix -t often derives a person, while -s derives a thing/object from adjectives or determiner stems.
The suffix -yan forms a collection, group, or -ary from noun stems.

derivation_word_building.title: Place and enclosure suffixes derive rooms, buildings, compartments, and boxes

rule.description: Place-like nouns are derived with suffixes such as -(e)m for place, -im for room, -am for house/building, -um for compartment/booth, and -yem for enclosure/box.
pseudocode: if target == general_place: use stem + 'em' or 'm'

derivation_word_building.title: Use -ar/-ir/-ur for tools, machines, engines, and vehicles

rule.description: The suffixes -ar, -ir, and -ur form instruments, tools, machines, engines, or vehicles, usually from verb stems. Exact nuance depends on the lexical family.

derivation_word_building.title: Science and theory suffixes derive fields and specialists

rule.description: -tun means science of/-ology; -tut means scientist/-ologist; -tuyn means pseudo-science; -tuin means theory.

derivation_word_building.title: Noun-to-adjective suffixes encode relation, fullness, lack, and likeness

rule.description: Noun-derived adjectives use -a for relating to/of, -aya or -ika for full of/-ful/-ous, -oya or -uka for lacking/-less/-free, and -yena for like/-ish/-y.

derivation_word_building.title: Verb-to-adjective endings form participles and potential meanings

rule.description: Verb-derived adjectives use -ea for active/present -ing, -yea for tending to/-ive, -wa for passive/past -ed, -oa for about to/apt to, and -ua for able/potentially causing.

translation_special_cases.title: Do not use ha for general abstractions, languages, countries, or names

rule.description: General concepts such as life, love, and freedom do not take ha unless referring to a specific instance. Names of languages, countries, and persons are already specific and normally do not take ha.

translation_special_cases.title: Omit English indefinite a/an in Mirad

rule.description: Mirad has no indefinite article equivalent to English a/an. An unmarked noun is indefinite or general unless made definite by ha or another determiner.

source.section: Modifying Nouns with the Definite Article

translation_special_cases.title: Default to gender-neutral forms unless gender is needed

rule.description: Mirad is gender-neutral by default. Use gender-specific nouns or pronouns only when English explicitly requires gender or when needed to distinguish referents.
pseudocode: apply w/y gender marking or lexical gendered form
